request.question: What 2012 South Korean action thriller film was turned into the 2014 Indian-Malay thriller Angels?

title: The Villainess
Confession of Murder
Tony Scott
No Tears for the Dead
The Chaser (film)
Tube (2003 film)
Ek Villain
A Company Man
Angels (2014 film)
Hwayi: A Monster Boy
sentences: The Villainess is a 2017 South Korean action thriller film directed by Jung Byung-gil, starring Kim Ok-bin.
The film had its world premiere at the 70th Cannes Film Festival in May 2017.
Confession of Murder (; lit.
"I am the Murderer") is a 2012 South Korean action thriller film directed by Jung Byung-gil, starring Jung Jae-young and Park Si-hoo.

It is about a police officer who is haunted for failing to capture a serial killer 15 years back, and returns to the case after a novelist publishes the book "I am the Murderer", claiming responsibility for the crimes.
Anthony David Leighton Scott (21 June 1944 – 19 August 2012) was an English film director and producer.

His films come from a broad range of genres, including the action drama "Top Gun" (1986), action comedy "Beverly Hills Cop II" (1987), auto racing film "Days of Thunder" (1990), action comedy "The Last Boy Scout" (1991), romantic dark comedy crime film "True Romance" (1993), submarine action film "Crimson Tide" (1995), psychological thriller "The Fan" (1996), spy thriller "Enemy of the State" (1998), spy film "Spy Game" (2001), action thriller "Man on Fire" (2004), sci-fi action thriller "Déjà Vu" (2006), thriller "The Taking of Pelham 123" (2009), and the action thriller "Unstoppable" (2010).

No Tears for the Dead (; literally Crying Man) is a 2014 South Korean action thriller film written and directed by Lee Jeong-beom, about a professional hitman (Jang Dong-gun) who is conflicted about killing his last target (Kim Min-hee).
The Chaser () is a 2008 South Korean action thriller film starring Kim Yoon-seok and Ha Jung-woo.
It was directed by Na Hong-jin in his directorial debut.
Inspired by real-life Korean serial killer Yoo Young-chul, the film was shot on location around Mangwon-dong in the Mapo District, Seoul.
Tube () is a 2003 South Korean action thriller film directed by Beak Woon-hak (or Baek Woon-hak).
The film features police officer Jay (Kim Suk-hoon) who is a subway police officer who spends his days reminiscing over his lost lover.
The pickpocket Kay (Bae Doona) becomes infatuated with Jay and tips him off about the government assassin Bishop (Park Sang-min) hijacking a subway car.
Both Jay and Kay find themselves in the target car when the Bishop makes his move.
Ek Villain (English: "One Villain") is a 2014 Indian romantic thriller directed by Mohit Suri.
The film stars Sidharth Malhotra, Shraddha Kapoor and Riteish Deshmukh in lead roles, and tells the story of a hardened criminal whose terminally ill wife is murdered by a serial killer.
The film was produced by Ekta Kapoor and Shobha Kapoor under Balaji Motion Pictures and ALT Entertainment.
The film is an unofficial remake of the 2010 South Korean action thriller film "I Saw the Devil".
Upon its release on 27 June 2014, the film received mixed to positive reviews from the critics, and was a commercial success.
A Company Man () is a 2012 South Korean action thriller film starring So Ji-sub, Lee Mi-yeon, Kwak Do-won and Kim Dong-jun.
It is about a hitman who find himself targeted by his ex-employers after he falls in love with a single mother and quits his job.
Angels is a 2014 Indian Malayalam social thriller film and the directorial debut of Jean Markose.
The film stars an ensemble cast consisting of Indrajith, Asha Sarath, Joy Mathew, Prem Prakash, Parvathy Menon, Baiju and Dinesh Panicker.
Story and screenplay were written by Jean Markose and Toni Tomy, dialogues were co-written by Shabu Kilithatil.
It is a rip-off from the South Korean film "Confession of Murder" (2012).
This film is a Moderated Success and Hit.

Hwayi: A Monster Boy () is a 2013 South Korean action thriller film about a 16-year-old boy of the same name (played by Yeo Jin-goo) who is raised by five criminal fathers to become the perfect assassin.
It takes pulling the trigger to discover his true identity after he realizes the mystery surrounding his past and his fate.
It was the highly anticipated second feature film by director Jang Joon-hwan, a decade after his 2003 cult favorite sci-fi comedy/thriller "Save the Green Planet!

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What 2012 South Korean action thriller film was turned into the 2014 Indian-Malay thriller Angels?
hop1_docs: Angels (2014 film) | Angels is a 2014 Indian Malayalam social thriller film and the directorial debut of Jean Markose.  The film stars an ensemble cast consisting of Indrajith, Asha Sarath, Joy Mathew, Prem Prakash, Parvathy Menon, Baiju and Dinesh Panicker.  Story and screenplay were written by Jean Markose and Toni Tomy, dialogues were co-written by Shabu Kilithatil.  It is a rip-off from the South Korean film "Confession of Murder" (2012).  This film is a Moderated Success and Hit.

Ek Villain | Ek Villain (English: "One Villain") is a 2014 Indian romantic thriller directed by Mohit Suri.  The film stars Sidharth Malhotra, Shraddha Kapoor and Riteish Deshmukh in lead roles, and tells the story of a hardened criminal whose terminally ill wife is murdered by a serial killer.  The film was produced by Ekta Kapoor and Shobha Kapoor under Balaji Motion Pictures and ALT Entertainment.  The film is an unofficial remake of the 2010 South Korean action thriller film "I Saw the Devil".  Upon its release on 27 June 2014, the film received mixed to positive reviews from the critics, and was a commercial success.

Holiday: A Soldier Is Never Off Duty | Holiday: A Soldier Is Never Off Duty is a 2014 Indian action thriller film written and directed by A. R. Murugadoss, and produced by Vipul Shah.  It features Akshay Kumar and Sonakshi Sinha in the lead roles, along with Farhad as the antagonist in his Bollywood debut and Govinda in a special appearance.  It is a remake of Murugadoss' own acclaimed Tamil film "Thuppakki" (2012).  The film released on 6 June 2014, becoming the biggest net grosser of the first half of 2014.  The movie was reported to be one of the best thrillers to come out of Bollywood.  It was also reported that Akshay Kumar's career took a new turn in terms of story selection and box office success with this movie.  Akshay Kumar was nominated for Filmfare Award for Best Actor at 60th Filmfare Awards.

Godfather (2012 film) | Godfather is a 2012 Indian Kannada Language action thriller musical film directed by cinematographer-turned- director Sethu Sriram starring Upendra and Soundarya Jayamala in the lead roles.  Catherine Tresa also plays a prominent role and Bhumika Chawla makes a special appearance.

Adah Sharma | Adah Sharma an Indian film actress who mainly appears in Hindi and Telugu language films.  Sharma, after finishing her schooling, made her acting debut with a leading role in the 2008 Hindi language horror film "1920", a box office success.  Her portrayal of a possessed woman in the film was critically praised and earned her the Filmfare Award for Best Female Debut nomination.  After the release of her critically and commercially successful romantic comedy film "Hasee Toh Phasee" (2014), she ventured into the South Indian film industries, where her first six films—five in Telugu language—the romantic thriller "Heart Attack" (2014), the drama "S/O Satyamurthy" (2015), the action comedy "Subramanyam for Sale" (2015), the romance "Garam" (2016) and the thriller "Kshanam" (2016) and one in Kannada— the action thriller "Rana Vikrama" (2015), achieved commercial success and garnered her critical appreciation for her performances.

A Hard Day | A Hard Day (; lit.  "Take It to the End") is a 2014 South Korean crime action thriller film written and directed by Kim Seong-hun, and starring Lee Sun-kyun and Cho Jin-woong.  It was selected to compete in the Directors' Fortnight section of the 2014 Cannes Film Festival.

Broken (2014 film) | Broken (; lit.  "Hovering Blade" or "Roving Edge") is a 2014 South Korean revenge action thriller film about a father (played by Jung Jae-young) who becomes a fugitive as he hunts down the two men responsible for his daughter's rape and murder.

hop2_docs: Angels (2014 film) | Angels is a 2014 Indian Malayalam social thriller film and the directorial debut of Jean Markose.  The film stars an ensemble cast consisting of Indrajith, Asha Sarath, Joy Mathew, Prem Prakash, Parvathy Menon, Baiju and Dinesh Panicker.  Story and screenplay were written by Jean Markose and Toni Tomy, dialogues were co-written by Shabu Kilithatil.  It is a rip-off from the South Korean film "Confession of Murder" (2012).  This film is a Moderated Success and Hit.

Confession of Murder | Confession of Murder (; lit.  "I am the Murderer") is a 2012 South Korean action thriller film directed by Jung Byung-gil, starring Jung Jae-young and Park Si-hoo.  It is about a police officer who is haunted for failing to capture a serial killer 15 years back, and returns to the case after a novelist publishes the book "I am the Murderer", claiming responsibility for the crimes.

Park Hae-il | Park Hae-il (born January 26, 1977) is a South Korean actor.  He began his acting career in theater, but soon gained the film industry's attention in 2003 with "Jealousy Is My Middle Name" and "Memories of Murder".  Park's film career took off, with leading roles in films of diverse genres, including relationship drama "Rules of Dating" (2005), horror mystery "Paradise Murdered" (2007), and crime thriller "Moss" (2010).  More recently, Park received Best Actor honors for his performance in the period action film "War of the Arrows", which was the highest-grossing Korean film of 2011.  He also received critical acclaim for his role as an aging poet in "A Muse" (2012).

Park Si-hoo | Park Si-hoo (born Park Pyeong-ho on April 3, 1978) is a South Korean actor.  He began his entertainment career as an underwear model and stage actor, then made his official television debut in 2005.  After several years in supporting roles, Park rose to fame in 2010 with the popular romantic comedy series "Queen of Reversals" and "Prosecutor Princess".  This was followed by leading roles in period drama "The Princess' Man" (2011) and thriller film "Confession of Murder" (2012).

Ha Jung-woo | Ha Jung-woo (born Kim Sung-hoon on March 11, 1978) is a South Korean actor, film director, screenwriter and film producer.  His breakthrough to stardom came with the role in Na Hong-jin's serial killer film "The Chaser" (2008).  One of the leading actors of his generation in Korean cinema, Ha Jung-woo showcased his versatility across movies from many genres: road movie "My Dear Enemy" (2008), sports film "Take Off" (2009), crime drama "The Yellow Sea" (2010), gangster saga "" (2012), romantic comedy "Love Fiction" (2012), spy thriller "The Berlin File" (2013), and action thriller "The Terror Live" (2013).  He made his directorial debut through the comedy film "Fasten Your Seatbelt" (2013), followed by "Chronicle of a Blood Merchant" (2015).

Broken (2014 film) | Broken (; lit.  "Hovering Blade" or "Roving Edge") is a 2014 South Korean revenge action thriller film about a father (played by Jung Jae-young) who becomes a fugitive as he hunts down the two men responsible for his daughter's rape and murder.
The Suspect (2013 South Korean film) | The Suspect () is a 2013 South Korean spy-action thriller film starring Gong Yoo, and directed by Won Shin-yun.
response.summary_2: Angels (2014) is a rip-off/adaptation of the 2012 South Korean action-thriller Confession of Murder. Confession of Murder is a 2012 South Korean action thriller directed by Jung Byung-gil and starring Jung Jae-young and Park Si-hoo (passage [2]).
